author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: What I'm exceptionally interested in, is the addition of graphene to Aircrete (tons of stuff on youtube about it) but it functionally infuses it with microbubbles using a soapy foam and turns a 40# bag of portland into 50 gallons of concrete.  It does reduce the compressive strength though, so it's limited to small structures.  I Really want to know if adding graphene could get it back up to at least close to normal compressive strength.  That would be an amazing savings in building costs, as it's also highly insulating as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: aircrete is mostly a foam and grapene wo't alter that so compressive strength is always going to be compromised but that doen't have to be a limitation only a consideration. A framework of cast beams or steel with aircrete infill would certainly allow for larger structures

author: @Kyle-sg4rm
text: Choice! I'm on a similar mission to make a cement material from scratch - Eg: Making calcium hydroxide from seashells, which can be used in the cement and to react with sodium/potassium carbonate (extracted from seaweed ash) to produce Sodium Hydroxide..... Dry Sodium Hydroxide and silica (could be desert sand) heated above 300 degrees C reportedly results in Sodium Silicate, which could be diluted, filtered and mixed with calcium hydroxide to produce Calcium Silicate Hydrate - which is a part of what makes portland cement strong. In absence of volcanic material, terracotta passes as being a Pozzolan, it could be crushed/powdered and used to help with water resistance etc. I've read that it was used in ancient cement for underwater applications. Also do you think it's possible that desert sand could be be 'etched' in an alkali in order to give it rougher surface?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suppose it is mate - but that would be a lot of waste alkali to get a useable amount

replies.author: @Kyle-sg4rm
replies.text: Instead of pouring it out, perhaps the waste alkali could also play other roles within the mix. By adding a form of cellulose along with urea (at a low temp), cellulose could be dissolved and a a sort of plastic could be produced - resulting in a geopolymer?

author: @everest3334

robert, it seems that these old video's will be extremely informative as regards generic 
Anti-Segregation of Sand Video
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L9V7E7oI_EY

especially as regards how all the nanomaterials will mix and Segregate, as in it appears that the finer the grain, the more it will settle over the centre and have the larger grains role off to the sides and stratify even more as the material is again poured and un-mixed, wow...
 
FMC Hopper Design- the correct way
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YnZnJ_u3u5w

and OC Mathematical Impressions: Spontaneous Stratification
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CpZaRn0Bez0
 could be a very useable option for making supercaps and batteries once you know the true reasons and automatic processes taking place ,great.

lots more to see and be informed, perhaps useful, i did find it informative in relation to many things you have instructed the reader to perform anyway.

author: @yougeo
text:
Fellow RMS subscribers Lets nominate Robert for official British/UK honours for his work of openly educating and sharing info with so many people worldwide in important new areas of technology to enable so many others to advance the science!  I think many of us recognize how unique he is in his willingness to share info and how much of an impact he has had all over the internet and the world. People who are working on graphene and supercapaciitors etc routinely recognize the influence Robert's videos have had on getting them started. 

I am not even from or in the UK but 
This appears to be the official site.  And don't have to be from the UK to nominate someone - in fact maybe it is even a better indication of  a nominee's influence.

https://www.gov.uk/honours/types-of-honours-and-awards

 that site says :"we need your nominee’s name, age, address and contact details details of relevant work or volunteering they’ve done details of any awards or other recognition they’ve received2 supporting letters to back up your nomination - these should be from people who know the nominee personally

You can include any evidence you have of recognition your nominee has
 received for their achievements, for example articles, photos or 
letters."

so robert 
i am filling this form out and somehow you need to get me or us your age and address.

other subscribers: even simple search on Robert Murray Smith will quickly show all the other people who have referenced R's influence on their own efforts to create things over the years. https://www.google.com/search?q=robert+murray+smith

for example https://forums.tesla.com/forum/forums/robert-murraysmith-world-first-10-kilofarad-capacitor-10000-f-made-edison-power

here is another from a mining consortium  http://graphite.com.co/how-to-make-graphene/

here is a bloomberg bit on him http://www.bloomberg.com/research/stocks/people/person.asp?personId=302886452&privcapId=283783528

but personally i think his influence on all the amateurs is what is really worthwhile for a nomination.

things like in general forums of people really testing the boundaries of science and tech like this http://overunity.com/16225/a-perspective-on-the-b-type-eesd-robert-murray-smith-any-issues/#.WVKFT-31G1E

hereis a really good example from 2013 https://jenslabs.com/2013/03/25/diy-graphene-videos/

I just think it would be great if he could get some official recognition for all the work he has done to help people. Taking nothing from Sir Robert Moore but all the Knights can't be movie stars because who will defend the realm in the apocalypse!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - that is so cool - lol - cheers

author: @johnmcfadden9336
text:
Would a high quality Graphene based  grout be blended with multi sized sand grains  spread out to dry but not fully cure then be gently damaged to break into  graded clusters  then allowed to cure fully. Chopped Feathers a big waste product could provide a useful addition with the right dosing  and applications  
If foamed cement was used as an external insulation on the high rise  blocks it would have cost a fraction in labour, environment, material costs and of cause lives  there is a fantastic range of applications for it and can greatly reduce the need for sand 
There is one company in America that has developed a foaming agent  that seems superior to others  unfortunately it's seems one would have to be like a franchise to get the stuff  
I hope to order some Eco foam soon and will try some of it out for FC  but it may be  a bit wasteful for that as there may be ingredients in it either not necessarily or optimal for the cement but you can use fairy liquid so it must be okay but perhaps not the best 
The idea of powered aluminium generating hydrogen bubbles if used not to make a pure foamed cement but the bubbles if extra strong from the Graphene  may help stabilise the mix while curing 
 Great pursuit rob and team  best of luck

replies.text: All of those are good ideas mate cheers

author: @agentbertram4769
text: You need sharp sand for building use.  Sharp sand has angular particles that bind together well.  It's like using rounded pebbles for a driveway.  They roll over each other, whereas angular crushed aggregate binds up when vibro-compacted.  Use sharp sand for best results mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @robioptic
text:
Interesting! :)

Try also Volcanic sand - its super strong for building. another thing that comes to mind is foam-concrete. 
I am guessing that your property for this test is surface area. N I am further guessing that graphite, being highly clingy and reactive [assumption :) needs more surface are and Bubbles are spheres which are incredibly strong n I am guessing again that anything which binds or improves the strength of a bubble wall will make it substantially stronger.

Foam concrete also uses no aggregate- so u get a simpler more accurate test to pin-down your results to a specific effect :)

1- foamed concrete
2- volcanic sharp sand  in foamed concrete
3- desert sand as u do now

It's almost a reversal of your current test hahaha :)

Looking forward to your next results :)
---

Also your battery technology looks so very exciting- would not mind supporting this project... cause as Watts said: I produce what the whole worlds wants. Power! hahah

Good luck :)

author: @numbereight886
text: I think you are barking up the wrong tree, concrete is cheap, it can already be strengthened. So why consider using a potentially expensive item to produce a marginal gain in concrete that could be achieved for less cost elsewhere. I thought initially you might have come up with the idea of turning concrete into a giant battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate we all have our ideas of what is the right and wrong direction - your judgements are made from lack of sufficient information  however - I don't mean this to be rude mate - just a fact - for example you don't know how much it costs us to make the graphene - it adds US$1.64 to the price of a cubic meter of concrete which costs around US$70 as it is - so - it's not that expensive - the gain in compressive strength is 94% - so, not that marginal.

replies.author: @numbereight886
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith - My father has used loads of concrete on the bases of oil containers on oil refineries, they have loads of engineers looking into strength of concrete. You say your concrete is 'stronger', my guess just in a compression sense. Have you tried substituting say rock dust for graphene and measured it's effect?  Now, you might possibly be onto something if say graphene forced out air bubbles in concrete etc. Personally I think you should be exploring some of your earlier ideas more...and taking them to market... then you could have a team working for you etc.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i know what you think mate - you made it clear - i just happen to disagree with you and as it is me who decides what i do - that's about all there is to say

replies.author: @jeffg6924
replies.text: not at all.  a lav Mic clips on your belt and you can put the mic under your collar.  no one will see it and it only takes a minute or two to sync the audio track when you post your video.  even a cheap $20 Mic will make you sound a million times better and will cancel most white noise in the background

author: @borisk6969
text: Sorry not many deserts in Yorkshire. I was watching a programme with Dick Strawbridge a few years back and he was using a waste glass product to make a heat sink for his greenhouse. The glass aggregate was made from old bottles - I think ultrasonic soundwaves were used to make a pea gravel sized product. I wonder if waste glass could be processed into sand sized/shaped grains, which could be used to create thermal concrete. I know waste glass is already used in thermal blocks, but as an aggregate rather than a sand. Just a thought

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Good thought and i Know Dick and remember when he did this. Your best bet, mate, is to run a few experiments it's not as hard as some folks think

if you wanted to improve dessert sand so that you could use it for building with concrete, do you think that if you could remove the polished surface of the grain of sand,  to get the surface required for a good bond, would an acid etch at least improve the situation?
Is there an acid that would "roughen up" the surface of a grain of round smooth desert sand, is it even possible?

replies.author: @jonathannumer5415
replies.text: the problem with adding a plant fibre into the mix is that it doesn't make it stronger. Adding fibre makes it lighter and it can regulate moisture better. Hempcrete has about 1/20th the compressive strength of regular concrete. It would be a lot of added resources. Hemp, as amazing as it is, still requires farming. The more hemp is used, the more hemp farms are required, and not all land is created equal for farming- either existing crops will be displaced or new untouched land will be displaced and water resources changed. This has a huge impact on the ecosystem. Turning more diverse forests into crops of hemp. The market controls how many other existing  crops it can displace. If Robert Smith's stronger concrete works out buildings will last longer- and if they use the coal for the graphene that will be more carbon trapped in stone again.

author: @gregoryalbright
text: Particle size variations add strength, perhaps as much as shape.  It might be the relative uniformity of the sand size is more important than shape?  Here in Vermont, we can order concrete with either ledge or river stone, the latter better for pumping as it flows better than the sharp ledge stone.   We can also spec. "pea stone". Either way the mix contains a range of sizes up to a particular screening.

author: @gregoryalbright
text:
Concrete thoughts to mix...
Turns out that the manufacturing of cement energy intensive and totally dependent on petroleum, and, so far there is no alternative for a post- petrol world.
An interesting concrete is made by the addition of aluminum powder, which apparently causes the concrete to foam as it sets resulting in a lightweight material.
Also, if your waste coal project takes off, there'll be another by-product from the combustion of coal aka 'conkers', or "fly-ash". This, another additive to cement, adds interesting qualities to concrete.
